92.timings: Kalamega Perumal Temple is located at Thirumoghur in the Pandya Nadu region, in a village a little to the north-east of Madurai, which serves as the nearest major city and transport hub with rail, road and air links. Buses and taxis from Madurai reach the village. As with most Vishnu temples, it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours with a break during midday. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

93.festivals: Thirukkottiyur, hallowed in Srivaishnava tradition as the place where Acharya Ramanuja received and openly shared the sacred Tirumantra, observes the annual Brahmotsavam as its principal festival, with the Lord Sowmya Narayana taken in procession on the customary vahanas amid Divya Prabandham recitation. Vaikunta Ekadasi during Margazhi is kept with special darshan, and the temple traditionally observes Pavithrotsavam and a Float festival (Theppotsavam). Days associated with Ramanuja and the great acharyas hold particular significance here given the temple's role in the disciplic tradition. The broader Vaishnava festival cycle, including Pongal, Tamil New Year, Krishna Jayanthi, Navaratri and Deepavali, is also marked with abhishekam, alankaram and devotional singing.
93.timings: Sowmya Narayana Perumal Temple lies at Thirukkottiyur in the Pandya Nadu region, within the Sivaganga district, set in a town some distance from Madurai, which is the nearest major city and transport hub; Sivaganga and Karaikudi are the closer towns served by road and rail. Buses and taxis reach Thirukkottiyur from these centres. Like most Vishnu temples, it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours with a midday closure. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

94.festivals: At this coastal shrine of Adi Jagannatha Perumal at Thiruppullani, the devotional calendar follows the well-attested Srivaishnava cycle. An annual Brahmotsavam is traditionally celebrated with great fervour, the utsava deity taken in procession on various vahanas through the temple streets. Vaikunta Ekadasi in the Tamil month of Margazhi is observed with the chanting of the Nalayira Divya Prabandham, the Pagal-pathu and Ra-pathu recitations, and the opening of the Paramapada Vasal for devotees. Pavithrotsavam, performed for the ritual purification of the year's worship, and the Float festival (Theppotsavam) on the temple tank are also traditionally kept. The temple's strong association with Sri Rama, who is said to have rested here on the darbha grass before crossing to Lanka, lends Ramayana-related observances a special devotional resonance.
94.timings: The temple stands at Thiruppullani, a coastal village in the Pandya Nadu region of Ramanathapuram district, Tamil Nadu, a short distance from Ramanathapuram town and on the route toward Rameswaram. Ramanathapuram serves as the nearest town with road and rail links, while Madurai is the larger city and transport hub for the area. Like most Vishnu temples, it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours with a midday closure. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

95.festivals: At the rock-cut shrine of Sathyamoorthy Perumal at Thirumeyyam, worship follows the traditional Srivaishnava festival cycle. An annual Brahmotsavam is celebrated with processions of the utsava murti on successive vahanas and special recitation of the sacred hymns. Vaikunta Ekadasi in Margazhi is kept with the chanting of the Nalayira Divya Prabandham and the Pagal-pathu and Ra-pathu observances. Pavithrotsavam, for the annual purification of the temple's rituals, and a Float festival (Theppotsavam) are also traditionally observed where the temple tank permits. The cave temple, with its reclining form of the Lord and its ancient inscriptions, draws devotees during these festival days for special abhishekams and alankarams. Where specific local observances are less widely documented, the temple maintains the common Vaishnava round of monthly Ekadasi and seasonal celebrations.
95.timings: The temple is located at Thirumeyyam in the Pudukkottai district of Tamil Nadu, within the Pandya Nadu region, set beside a fortified hill near the Pudukkottai-Karaikudi road. Pudukkottai is the nearest town with rail and road connections, while Tiruchirappalli and Madurai are the larger cities and transport hubs serving the area. As with most Vishnu temples, it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours with a break around midday. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

96.festivals: As the celebrated birthplace of Sri Rama, Ayodhya observes a devotional calendar centred on the Lord's life. Sri Rama Navami, marking the birth of Rama in the month of Chaitra, is the foremost festival, drawing vast gatherings for elaborate worship, processions and the recitation of the Ramayana. Vijayadasami, recalling Rama's victory, and Deepavali, commemorating his triumphant return to Ayodhya after exile, are kept with great splendour and the lighting of countless lamps along the Sarayu river. In keeping with the Srivaishnava tradition that venerates Raghunayagan among the Divya Desams, devotees also observe Vaikunta Ekadasi and the chanting of the Tamil Prabandham. Annual Brahmotsavam-style processions, ceremonial bathing of the deity, and the festive recitation of Rama's glories mark the principal occasions of the year.
96.timings: The temple is at Ayodhya (Thiruayodhi) on the banks of the Sarayu river in Uttar Pradesh, in the far northern region honoured among the Divya Desams. Ayodhya lies close to Faizabad and is connected by rail and road, with Lucknow serving as the nearest major city and air hub. Like most Vishnu temples, worship is generally offered during morning and evening hours, with timings often extended on major festival days. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

97.festivals: As one of the northern Divya Desams revered in the Nalayira Divya Prabandham, the observances here follow the broad Srivaishnava festival cycle rather than the elaborate Tamil temple calendar of the southern shrines. The annual Brahmotsavam, when celebrated, honours Devaraja Perumal with processions and special abhishekams, and Vaikunta Ekadasi is traditionally kept as a day of fasting and darshan. Sri Rama Navami, Krishna Janmashtami, and the great Vaishnava Ekadasis are marked here, fitting for a place celebrated in the Puranas as Naimisaranyam, the forest where sages gathered to hear sacred recitation. Pilgrims often combine their visit with the wider sanctity of this tirtha rather than with a single named utsavam.
97.timings: Naimisaranyam (Naimisharanya) lies in Sitapur district of Uttar Pradesh in the northern region, near the Gomti river, roughly northwest of Lucknow, the nearest major city and transport hub with rail and air connections. The temple is set within this ancient pilgrimage forest area. Like most Vishnu temples, it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours with a midday break. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

98.festivals: Thiruppiridhi is a Himalayan Divya Desam glorified by the Azhwars, and its observances reflect the simpler devotional rhythm of these remote northern shrines rather than a full Tamil utsavam calendar. The principal Vaishnava occasions are traditionally honoured here: Vaikunta Ekadasi as a day of fasting and special darshan, the major annual Ekadasis, Sri Rama Navami, and Krishna Janmashtami, when Vasudeva Perumal receives special worship. Where a Brahmotsavam is observed, the Lord is honoured with abhishekam and ritual offerings. Because the shrine sits within the wider sacred geography of the upper Himalayas, pilgrims commonly venerate it as part of the larger Char Dham circuit, and its sanctity is tied to that pilgrimage season as much as to any single festival.
98.timings: Thiruppiridhi is identified with a Himalayan site in the Uttarakhand region of the northern hills, in the vicinity of the upper Alaknanda valley near Devaprayag, far from the southern temple towns. The nearest large city and transport hub is Rishikesh or Dehradun, from which mountain roads continue upward. Like most Vishnu temples, it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours, subject to the seasonal access of the high Himalayas. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

99.festivals: At Devaprayag, the Thirukkandam Divya Desam praised by the Azhwars, worship of Purushothaman follows the devotional pattern of the Himalayan shrines rather than an elaborate Tamil utsavam cycle. The great Vaishnava observances are traditionally kept here: Vaikunta Ekadasi and the principal annual Ekadasis as days of fasting and darshan, Sri Rama Navami, and Krishna Janmashtami, when the Lord receives special abhishekam and offerings. The temple's setting at the confluence of the Bhagirathi and Alaknanda, where the Ganga is born, lends it a deep tirtha sanctity, and pilgrims commonly venerate it within the seasonal Char Dham pilgrimage rather than for a single named festival. Where a Brahmotsavam is observed, the deity is honoured with processions and ritual worship.
99.timings: Devaprayag (Thirukkandam) stands in the Tehri Garhwal area of Uttarakhand in the northern hill region, at the sacred confluence of the Bhagirathi and Alaknanda rivers that forms the Ganga. The nearest major hubs are Rishikesh and Dehradun, from which hill roads lead up to the town. Like most Vishnu temples, it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours, subject to seasonal mountain access. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

100.festivals: As a high Himalayan shrine of the Lord reclining as Badri Narayanan, the temple's life follows the rhythm of the mountains. Worship is possible only during the open season, and its great moments are the ceremonial opening of the gates in the spring, when the deity is bathed and adorned, and the solemn closing before winter snows. Within the season, devotees traditionally observe the Vaishnava cycle of festivals, including the annual Brahmotsavam, Vaikunta Ekadasi, and special abhishekams and recitation of sacred hymns. The Lord, glorified by the Azhwars as one of the few divine seats of the Vada Nadu, draws pilgrims for the Char Dham yatra, and large gatherings mark the seasonal opening and closing rites observed with great devotion.
100.timings: The temple stands at Badrinath in the Himalayas of the northern region, set high in the upper Alaknanda valley and approached through Uttarakhand's mountain roads from larger towns and the nearest rail and airheads in the plains below. Being a high-altitude shrine, it opens only seasonally, and within that season it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours like most Vishnu temples. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

101.festivals: At this revered Himalayan kshetra, where the Lord is worshipped as Mukti Narayanan amid the sacred Salagrama setting, devotion centres on the bestowal of liberation that gives the place its name. Pilgrims traditionally observe the broad Vaishnava festival cycle, including Vaikunta Ekadasi and the annual celebrations honouring Narayana, alongside ritual bathing at the holy springs and abhishekams to the Lord. Being a remote mountain shrine sacred to many traditions, its observances are shaped by season and pilgrimage, with worship most active in the milder months. The Azhwars' glorification of this northern seat draws Srivaishnava pilgrims who keep the festival days with hymn recitation and offerings in a spirit of seeking moksha.
101.timings: The temple lies at Salagramam (Muktinath) high in the mountains of Nepal, in the northern region, reached by long pilgrim routes through the Himalayas from the nearest towns and transport hubs serving the area. As a high-altitude shrine, access depends on the season, and within open periods it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours like most Vishnu temples. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

102.festivals: Honoured as the birthplace of the Lord who appears here as Bala Krishnan, this northern kshetra naturally makes Krishna Janmashtami its most beloved festival, when the divine child's advent is celebrated through midnight worship, abhishekam, and joyous song. Devotees also traditionally keep the wider Vaishnava cycle, including the annual Brahmotsavam, Vaikunta Ekadasi, and festivals recalling the Lord's childhood leelas at Mathura and the surrounding land. Glorified by the Azhwars as a sacred seat of the Vada Nadu, the place draws pilgrims who recite the Tamil hymns and offer worship with deep devotion. Throughout the year, the cycle of abhishekams and processions keeps the memory of Krishna's avatara vivid for those who gather here.
102.timings: The temple is located at Mathura in Uttar Pradesh, within the northern region, set in the historic Krishna country along the Yamuna and well connected to Delhi and other major cities by the region's rail and road networks. Like most Vishnu temples it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours, with worship continuing through the day on major festival occasions. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

103.festivals: At this Krishna kshetram in the Braj country, the great annual cycle of Sri Krishna's life is observed with deep devotion, above all Sri Krishna Janmashtami, when the Lord's midnight birth is celebrated with abhishekam, song and rejoicing. Govardhan Puja and the autumn festivals associated with Krishna's childhood at Gokul are also kept with fervour in the surrounding region. In the Srivaishnava tradition the temple honours the usual sacred days of the Vishnu calendar, including a Brahmotsavam, Vaikunta Ekadasi in the cold season, and Pavithrotsavam for ritual purification. Through the year the festivals recall Krishna's playful childhood among the cowherds, drawing pilgrims who come to behold the Lord praised by the Azhwars as Navamohana Krishnan.
103.timings: The temple stands at Gokul (Thirukkokulam) in the Braj region near Mathura, in the northern Vada Nadu tradition of Uttar Pradesh, set in the Krishna heartland beside the Yamuna. Mathura is the nearest major city and transport hub, well connected by road and rail, with Agra and Delhi the larger gateways further out. Like most Vishnu temples it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours, with a midday interval. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

104.festivals: As one of Sri Krishna's principal abodes, this Dwarka kshetram observes Sri Krishna Janmashtami as its foremost festival, marking the Lord's birth with abhishekam, adornment and night-long devotion. The Braj and Gujarat traditions surrounding Krishna as king of Dwarka are recalled through the year, and the great pilgrim seasons draw large gatherings to the seaside shrine. In keeping with Srivaishnava observance the temple honours the wider Vishnu festival cycle, including a Brahmotsavam, Vaikunta Ekadasi during Margazhi, and Pavithrotsavam. Festival days unfold with processions, recitation of sacred verse and special offerings, celebrating the Lord whom the Azhwars revered as Kalyana Narayanar, the Lord of auspicious marriage.
104.timings: The temple is at Dwarka (Thiruthuvarakai) on the western coast of Gujarat, a celebrated pilgrim town at the edge of the Arabian Sea in the northern Vada Nadu tradition. Dwarka has its own railway station on the western line, with Jamnagar and Rajkot the nearer cities and airports serving the region. Like most Vishnu temples it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours, with a break during the day. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

105.festivals: At this celebrated Narasimha kshetram the festivals centre on the man-lion avatara and His devotee Prahlada. Narasimha Jayanti is observed with great devotion, recalling the Lord's fierce manifestation to save His child-devotee, and the annual Brahmotsavam draws crowds across the Eguva (upper) and Diguva (lower) Ahobilam shrines. A grand celestial wedding festival of the Lord with Chenchu Lakshmi, cherished by the local Chenchu community, is among the most famous observances here. The temple also keeps Vaikunta Ekadasi and other days of the Vishnu calendar, with processions through the forested hills. As the seat associated with the Ahobila Mutt, festival seasons are rich with Vedic recitation, prabandham and the worship of the nine forms of Narasimha.
105.timings: The temple lies at Ahobilam (Singavel Kundram) amid the forested Nallamala hills of Andhra Pradesh, in the northern Vada Nadu tradition, spread across the lower (Diguva) and upper (Eguva) shrines. The town of Allagadda is the nearest base, with Nandyal and Kurnool the closer cities and transport hubs reached by road and rail. Like most Vishnu temples it generally observes morning and evening darshan hours, with a midday interval. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

106.festivals: Tirumala observes one of the richest festival calendars among all Vishnu temples. The foremost is the annual Brahmotsavam dedicated to Lord Venkateswara, traditionally celebrated over nine days with grand processions in which the utsava deity, Malayappa Swami, is taken around on a succession of magnificent vahanas, the Garuda Sevai being the most thronged. Vaikunta Ekadasi is observed with deep devotion, when pilgrims seek darshan through the sacred Vaikunta Dwaram. Other traditionally cherished observances include the Float festival (Theppotsavam) on the temple tank, Pavithrotsavam for ritual purification, Rathasapthami, and the annual Pushpa Yagam and Pushpa Pallaki. Daily and weekly sevas such as the Suprabhatam, Thomala, Archana, and Kalyanotsavam draw devotees throughout the year, making the hill a continuous centre of worship.
106.timings: The temple crowns the Tirumala hills, a short ascent above the town of Tirupati in Andhra Pradesh's Chittoor area, in the region revered as Vada Nadu. Tirupati town below is well connected by rail and road to Chennai and other major southern cities and has its own airport, with road and footpath routes leading up to the Tirumala shrine. Being among the most visited temples, it observes extended morning and evening darshan hours, though crowds and queue systems vary greatly. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

107.festivals: Thiruppaarkadal, the Ocean of Milk, is a celestial Divya Desam sung by the Azhwars rather than an earthly temple, so it has no fixed annual festival calendar of its own. It is venerated as the abode where Lord Ksheerabdhi Nathan reclines upon Adisesha attended by Sri Mahalakshmi. Devotees honour this divine abode especially during the sacred month of Karthikai through the observance of Ksheerabdhi Dwadasi, the day commemorating the churning of the Ocean of Milk, when Tulasi and the Lord are worshipped together. More broadly, the great Vaishnava observances such as Vaikunta Ekadasi and Tirumanjanam are recalled here in spirit, since this milk-ocean is held to be the source from which the Lord descends into His earthly shrines.
107.timings: Thiruppaarkadal is not a geographical location but a celestial Divya Desam, the cosmic Ocean of Milk extolled in the hymns of the Azhwars as the reclining abode of Sri Maha Vishnu. Having no earthly coordinates, it cannot be visited as a physical temple; it is contemplated through prayer, recitation, and the worship offered at the Lord's earthly shrines. Devotees wishing to honour this abode may do so at established Vishnu temples that celebrate the milk-ocean tradition. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.

108.festivals: Sri Vaikuntam, the supreme abode of Paramapadam, is the highest celestial Divya Desam praised by the Azhwars, not an earthly temple, and so observes no annual festival cycle of its own. It is revered as the eternal realm where Lord Sri Vaikuntanathan is seated in splendour with Sri, Bhu and Neela Devi, attended by the liberated souls and the Nitya Suris. Its glory is recalled above all on Vaikunta Ekadasi, when major Vishnu temples open the Paramapada Vasal, the gateway symbolising entry into this very abode, and devotees pass through it seeking the Lord's eternal grace. The daily recitation of the Tiruvaymozhi and the Naalayira Divya Prabandham keeps the remembrance of Paramapadam ever alive among the faithful.
108.timings: Sri Vaikuntam, or Paramapadam, is the transcendent celestial Divya Desam, the eternal supreme abode beyond the material worlds, and therefore has no earthly location or coordinates. It cannot be reached by road, rail, or air, but is contemplated as the ultimate goal of the devotee, approached through devotion, surrender, and the grace of the Lord. Its symbolic gateway, the Paramapada Vasal, is opened at earthly Vishnu temples on Vaikunta Ekadasi for darshan. Verify current darshan timings and travel details with the temple or official sources before visiting.
